data.title: TRX Gold Is A Conditional Buy (A Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My buy judgment for TRX Gold Corporation (TRX) is a highly conditional buy. After 4 years of a “dead” zone period despite increasing gold prices, the gold producer started delivering interesting results, becoming fully self-funded (zero debt on the balance sheet), publishing an uncertain (but plausible) PEA assessment of 62,000 oz/year average gold production during the 17.6 years of the mine’s estimated life, and discovering the Stamford Bridge Zone.

My Monte Carlo NAV simulation (under some fundamental assumptions made) estimated a median share price of $0.69, significantly below the current share price of $1.50. Looking solely at the numbers, this would be enough to justify a Sell rating, but the positive skewness of the stock justifies the bullish investment for an investor convinced of three assumptions: 3-year investment horizon (to be aligned with the beginning of Phase 2 of the PEA), sustained gold prices above $3,000/oz and risk tolerance for an investment with huge concentration in a single country such as Tanzania.

In other words, investing in TRX makes sense only if an experienced investor is looking for a higher exposure to gold prices. On the contrary, an investor looking for a margin-of-safety gold exposure, the comparative analysis of TRX against SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) and VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) will show how these ETFs represent a better investment.

Introduction and Business Overview

TRX Gold (NYSE: TRX) is a junior gold producer, currently operating a single gold asset, which is the Buckreef Gold Project, located in Tanzania. The difference between being a gold producer and a gold developer or explorer is key, as in the first case I could rely on audited financial data published on Yahoo Finance to derive my analysis.

TRX has completed an important expansion of its production and processing plants for the Buckreef project, which enabled the company to increase its productivity to 2,000 tonnes per day.

Last year the company published a Preliminary Economic Assessment (PEA), a key economic/engineering document for gold producers that analyzes and provides estimates for the following scheduled expansion phases. It is interesting looking at some metrics I find insightful:

Particularly interesting is the assumed NPV at 5%, which is incredibly low for the producer. It is worth underlining that the PEA cannot be considered as a feasibility study, but only as a draft analysis based on inferred resources, which are per se characterized by high geological uncertainty and estimated using less rigorous engineering techniques.

Going on to the financial data published in the last Annual Report of the company, we understand that the company was able to sell 19,213 oz of gold, with an average realized price of $3,033/oz, which brought the company to obtain $57.6M in revenue (an all-time record for TRX).

The gross profit stands at 41.6%, with an EBITDA of $22.0M (38.1% EBITDA margin) and an operating cash flow of $16.3M. It’s interesting looking at the current net debt position of the company of $4.6M, basically debt-free. The growth capital required over the next 4 years is approximately $89M, which is ~$22M on an annual basis, 41% more than the current CapEx of $15.6M. This means that the company will require a significant increase in investment to actually be able to deploy its gold resources.

The production guidance for FY2026 is between 25,000 and 30,000 ounces of gold, which represents a minimum increase of roughly 31.5% of the 2025 gold production of 18,935 ounces. We must underline that the company reported a 6,550 oz production only in Q1 of FY2026, also an all-time record for TRX Gold.

The adjusted EBITDA also grew a lot in the latest published report to ~53% margin, with an operating cash flow coming in at 16% of revenues and an average realized price of $3,860 per ounce, as reported by the management in the last earnings call in January. This higher-than-expected cash result enables the company to reinvest more in the production of gold, which I expect to increase in the following quarter by 5-10%, realizing another record for TRX.

I want to proceed with my analysis looking at the stock performance from a quantitative and statistical point of view, which will be the main point that will lead to the valuation of the stock.

Statistical Valuation

All financial and market data used are from Yahoo Finance.

Below we can observe how TRX Gold has performed in the last 5 years in the financial markets, together with the performance of two key ETFs for our case:

  • GDL, the main ETF for gold
  • GDX, the main ETF tracking gold miners performance

Both the GLD and the GDX follow almost identical paths, with relatively stable periods until the spike began in early-2025. TRX Gold’s stock also follows a similar path, but the spike is lagged towards the end of 2025. The pricing follows what can be defined more as an option payoff, with periods of very stable movements followed by surprising and violent increases in price. Statistically, TRX is a stock with considerable excess kurtosis and positive skewness in the returns, meaning that the usual ratio computed to value the risk-adjusted performance of a stock, such as the Sharpe Ratio, the Calmar Ratio or the Sortino Ratio, needs to be adjusted, as they assume reasonable normality in the returns of the stock valued.

This assumption is confirmed by more in-depth analysis, and the chart above clearly shows it: TRX has a skewness of 1.94, which is extremely high for a financial series like stock returns (most stocks have skewness between -0.5 and +0.5). Practically, TRX shows payoffs that can be considered similar to the ones of a venture capital asset, in which on average you lose small-to-medium amounts of money frequently, but you can earn a lot rarely.

Special considerations can be made also for the kurtosis of 5.45: TRX has tails much fatter than a “normal” stock, which means that extreme events, occur much more frequently. In the chart it is possible to see returns between 0.5 and 0.8, these events would be practically impossible under the normality assumption.

Such considerations are completely different for GLD and GDX. The first one shows, practically, a normal behavior, with almost zero skewness (0.61) and negligible excess kurtosis, something expectable by a very high liquid asset like gold, mainly driven by macroeconomics. GDX sits more in the middle, as it is an ETF composed of the main gold miners and producers and, therefore, is a very diversified asset. Still, we can observe some right skewness, but this skewness resembles much more a Normal distribution.

Both the Jarque-Bera and the Shapiro-Wilk normality test strongly reject the normality assumption for TRX (as expected), while confirming it for the two ETFs considered.

Analysing the distribution statistic, we can see how TRX was positive in returns for exactly 50% of the months considered, but, nonetheless, the stock was able to deliver a positive CAGR due to the extremely positive returns it had in those bullish months (the max return was 77.17%, while the minimum was -30.39%). On the other hand, GDX was positive in 59.46% of the months, but delivered only a slightly higher CAGR.

It is statistically important to observe the effect of the volatility drag for TRX, which is equal to the variance of the stock/index divided by two. For TRX it is 16.33% (actual, 20.86% estimated assuming a symmetric normal distribution), and this volatility drag has a negative effect on the mean return of 30.38% (annualized) for the stock, giving back, at the end of the day, just a 14.05% return on an annual basis. The volatility drag effect is much higher for TRX than for the ETFs considered:

  • GLD, which has a 18.04% average annual return, suffers from only a 0.03% volatility drag
  • GDX, which has a 25.93% average annual return, suffers from a 5.24% volatility drag

Both ETFs have outperformed TRX on a CAGR basis, even with lower mean monthly returns.

I also wanted to test whether TRX (together with GDL and GDX) has, statistically speaking, a significant lag-1 autocorrelation. In other words, I wanted to test if a good month for the stock tends to followed by another good month in terms of returns, basically confirming a possible momentum strategy valid for both the stock and the ETFs. The results are in the table below:

Basically, the verdict was negative for all of them, meaning that the monthly returns of the stock are independent. This discovery practically prohibits us from relying on a solid momentum strategy. The most confident way (yet not precise) to value TRX would be with a fundamental analysis based on the NAV.

Risk-Adjusted Metrics

Before looking into the NAV model, I would like to investigate some risk-adjusted metrics (where possible, given the non-normal distribution of the stock).

Looking at the risk-adjusted metrics, it is clear how TRX underperformed both the GLD and the GDX by all measures in the full-period considered. In the breakdown period, the stock performed slightly better than GLD and GDX on the Sortino Ratio and outperformed only GDX on the Calmar Ratio. In other words, for the Sortino Ratio in the full period, TRX delivered 0.9360 unit of excess risk (above the risk free rate of 2.79%) for every unit of only downside risk accepted; for the Calmar ratio in the full period, TRX compensated its investors at a 0.2042 rate for drawdown risk, more than 8 times less than GLD.

Going through the maximum drawdown dates, I had proof of something that might sound obvious, but that is worth reminding: When during August 2022 and February 2025 the gold was recovering in price (both GDL and GDX were going up in price), TRX was continuing to head down. This is evidence of the fact that the market never prices gold producers for gold price alone, but:

  1. Firstly, it has to be certain of an operational execution of the gold producer
  2. Secondly, even when the gold producer is operative, it always prices idiosyncratic risk, mainly associated with possible operational disruptions (due to country risks and natural events, among all)

In order to find a risk-adjusted performance metric not relying on the return distribution assumption, I computed the Omega Ratio, which has at the numerator the probability-weighted average of the returns of the stock above a certain specified threshold and at the denominator the probability weighted average of the returns below the threshold t. I choose the risk-free rate as a threshold in order to correctly compare the Omega Ratio to the Calmar and Sortino Ratios.

The outcome of the analysis shows how TRX performs at the bottom in the full period and ranks second out of three behind GLD in the breakout period. Given the respected normality assumption in the Omega Ratio, I would consider this metric as more reliable than the Sortino Ratio.

Operational Leverage Analysis

To bridge the final gap between the historical analysis of the stock and the NAV model for valuation purposes, I wanted to look into one of the most important metrics for gold producers: the Operating Leverage, which is a ratio that measures how much a gold producer’s revenue and cash flow change for a given change in the price of gold.

It is possible to estimate this leverage using an empirical analysis based on a regression: regressing TRX’s EBITDA changes (from the company’s financial reports obtained through Yahoo Finance) against gold prices.

First of all, as measured by the R-squared, the regression performed explains 78.4% of the variability of the EBITDA using only one variable: the gold price. Just this metric is evidence of the strength of the relationship between the main proxy of cash generated for TRX (EBITDA) and gold prices, the 21.6% left is likely attributable to management choices, volume changes, and cost fluctuations.

The relationship between the variables is statistically significant (p-value ~0) and it is telling us that every $1/oz increase in the price of gold is associated with an increase in quarterly EBITDA of $5,561 ($22,245 on annual basis).

We can also infer what the gold price is for which TRX makes zero EBITDA, and this metric is roughly $1,674/oz. With the current gold price, this is not much of a problem, but it is a risk that should be kept in mind for an investor in the company.

I decided to compute what I think is one of the most important tables in the analysis: the Cash Margin Sensitivity Table. It shows what would be the cash impact on TRX of both a decline and an increase in gold prices.

Given the expected 2026 production guidance, the current gold price, the cash cost of the company, and its current cash margin:

  • A decrease of gold price by -$500/oz would reduce the annual cash flows by -$13.75M (84% reduction from the current number)
  • A decrease of gold price by -$200/oz would reduce the annual cash flow by -$5.5M
  • An increase of gold price by $100/oz would increase operating cash flows up to 2.8M
  • An increase of gold price by $500/oz would increase TRX’s cash flows by $13.8M

The company can range between seeing its cash flows obliterated and being able to almost cover its PEA expansion CapEx of $89M only with internal cash. That’s the real-world effect of the statistical property of excess kurtosis and positive skewness that I showed before.

Valuation and NAV Model

The core idea of the NAV model is simple: building a DCF model discounting the cash flow of an asset (the mine) that has a finite number of years (the mine’s life).

Building on the guidance given by the management, we can distinguish and separate two operational phases:

  • Phase 1, that goes from FY2026 to FY2028, with gold production between 25,000 and 30,000 oz/year, being extracted at 2,000 tonnes-per-day rate;
  • Phase 2, that goes from FY2029 to the end of the life of the mine, with gold production ramping up until 62,000 oz/year. For this explicit period I will use the assumptions reported on the PEA.

The ramping of the gold’s production will be linear, while costs for Phase 2 are assumed to be lower than Phase 1, basing on the fact that mining at more depth levels is more cost-efficient per ounce of gold, as TRX will be able to spread fixed cost on a larger production.

The CapEx for expansion activities, as I said above, is assumed to be $89M spread over 4 years, I assumed the following division:

  • Year 2, $25M
  • Year 3, $30M
  • Year 4, $25M
  • Year 5, $9M

For tax and royalties assumptions, I estimated, according to Tanzania’s fiscal regime on mining activities, the following: export royalty of 7.35% of revenues, domestic sales royalty of 4.35%, corporate tax rate of 30%.

The WACC requires more specific assumptions, given the specific nature of TRX’s business model.

Staring with the risk-free rate, I decided to use the 20-year US Treasury bond, as it has the right duration that correctly matches the estimated cash flows of the company in the model.

For the Equity Risk Premium (ERP), I decided to follow Damodaran’s approach, summing the US’s ERP (4.60%) and Tanzania’s Country Risk Premium (CRP of 6.25%), built on the default spread of the company (4.75%), the mining risk estimated by the Fraser Institute (1.00%) and the historical precedent of the 2017 Mining Act (0.5%). Since TRX has all its business operations located in Tanzania, the company is exposed to 100% of this CRP.

For the beta, I relied again on Damodaran’s industry benchmark for gold (0.85) for all scenarios in order to avoid any personal judgment on the stock’s sensitivity to the market (which was very unstable).

To account for the relatively small size of the company, I use the Duff & Phelps Size Premium Study (2025) to compute a small company premium of 3.37%.

Finally, I decided to compute a Project Stage Premium (PSP), which is a very specific risk component for mining companies. It accounts for the fact that different stages of production carry an amount of uncertainty coming from the state of the engineering and geological work made in the mine. Investors must be rewarded for this risk. Phase 1 (30% of the mine’s life) carries no PSP, as the estimates made by the management on production activities are well reliable and based on past production data. Phase 2 (70% of the mine’s life) is based on PEA assumptions, which are per se uncertain. To estimate the PSP for this phase of the project, I used CIM Guidelines on discount rates, which imply a 3% additional risk associated with estimates made on a PEA. Using a blended approach (0% PSP for 30% of the mine’s life and 3% for the remaining 70%), the final PSP is 2.1%.

Total WACC, which converges to the cost of equity given that TRX has no debt, is summarized in the following table:

For reference, I added the following sensitivity table, showing how the WACC would change if we modified the assumptions we made:

For the estimation of the NAV model, I used a Monte Carlo simulation. It means that I estimated a probability distribution of TRX’s estimated share price, starting with assumptions made on the probability distribution of the inputs.

For gold price, I assumed a Geometric Brownian Motion (GBM), simulating 20 years of possible gold price paths, which is far more realistic than assuming a static distribution of this input.

The All-in Sustaining Cost (AISC), is the fundamental cost metric for gold producers, and it is essentially a sum of all cost components divided by the ounces produced. For the distribution of this input, I assumed a mean-reverting process, as it historically reverts to the long-run cost level, determined by the global mining cost curve.

For the production input I choose a log-normal distribution, as production cannot be negative for a gold producer, correcting for more uncertainty expected in Phase 2.

For the WACC, I assumed a uniform distribution between 15% and 19.17% (my estimate). This means that in each scenario computed, the WACC can assume all the values in the range with equal probability.

To address the fact that gold prices directly drive 2 of the other three inputs (AISC and production), I computed a correlation matrix relying on the 13 quarterly observations from Yahoo Finance. It is important for the reader to acknowledge that 13 observations are not enough to establish a meaningful statistical relationship between the variables, but in this case I considered it more honest than making arbitrary assumptions on the inputs’ correlation. The results are shown in the picture below:

The only statistically significant relationship here is the one between AISC and production.

Coming to the final output of the Monte Carlo simulation:

The median NAV per share obtained in the simulation is $0.69, which is significantly below the current share price pf $1.26. Mathematically, this result is telling us that $0.69 is the value such that 50% of the simulated scenarios give us a higher share price and the other 50% give us a lower intrinsic value. Under the assumption made, this would be the “fair” price of the stock.

The current market valuation is $1.50, in our model this number corresponds to something between the 75th percentile and the 90th percentile. This means that roughly 75-90% of all simulations made by the model under my assumptions produce a share price lower than what is currently determined by the market.

Note that the difference between a Gaussian Monte Carlo simulation and a t-copula Monte Carlo simulation is negligible.

Looking at the Tornado Chart adds more value to our results:

The table tells us how much each input contributes to the determination of the NAV and the direction of this contribution. Gold price is the main positive driver, 2.5 times more important than AISC. This means that our positive scenarios are mainly driven by bullish gold paths.

AISC and production are equally important, but in different directions, and this is intuitive.

Interestingly, the WACC is the least important input, roughly one-fifth as important as the gold price.

Limits and Risks

There are some limitations and risks around the thesis expressed, mainly centered on the statistical and mathematical methods used.

First of all, we saw how the stock performances are divided into two periods: one “dead” period before August 2024 and the breakout period immediately after. These two regimes produced structurally different metrics, and the overall statistics are essentially a weighted average of the two periods. Neither metrics considering only the “dead” period nor the ones focused on the breakout period are enough to completely describe the stock’s behaviour.

Moreover, I faced some statistical uncertainty in estimating some key results, such as the Ljung-box test for the breakout period and the correlation matrix in the Monte Carlo simulation. This uncertainty is directly related to the small amount of observations available, which introduced wide confidence intervals for the estimates.

The stock price distribution obtained using the Monte Carlo method, then, is only as accurate as the assumed distribution of the inputs (gold price, AISC, production, and WACC). As I did my best in assuming distributions with a good tradeoff of complexity and accuracy, I cannot guarantee that they are the best mathematical models.

The model itself is largely based on the PEA assumptions made by the management, which has a high degree of uncertainty. As I tried to incorporate this risk into the WACC inside the model, there still is a possibility of discovering reserves and resources that are incompatible with what has been reported in the PEA.

After these statistical limits, there are, of course, risks more directly related to the nature of the investment, which is basically a leveraged bet on gold performance. Gold price at $2,500/oz stands at one standard deviation downside scenario if we consider a 3-year timeframe, according to my GBM model. This means that a decline in gold prices of such magnitude is not a tail or extreme risk, but it is fairly plausible. I have to underline, then, how TRX is not a major diversified gold producer, but it is currently operating in a single region (Tanzania), and any single disruptive event (both natural and political risks are possible in such a risky country) can affect the stock price in an incredibly negative way.

Conclusion

In my opinion, at the current price level, the stock deserves a conditional Buy rating. The quantitative valuation of TRX’s stock has demonstrated how it is currently overpriced by the market and how the stock itself delivered risk-adjusted returns lower than more diversified ETFs in the same sector. At the same time, the positive skewness of the stock’s returns shows how investing in TRX still makes sense if an investor is confident in sustained gold prices.

data.title: Broadcom: The AI Plumber Set For A Massive Explosion
data.description:
Investment Rating - "Buy"

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) develops custom AI accelerators called XPUs, Ethernet networking, and also runs an infrastructure software business (VMware), thus playing the role of an AI plumber for the entire AI infrastructure buildout, so to speak. Logically, the current AI boom drove up AVGO's quotes, but even before OpenAI's (OPENAI) first GPT tech was presented to the public, AVGO was a steady compounder for its investors. This year is shaping up differently, as Broadcom is down by ~24% from its ATH, hitting the depth of the sell-offs we've seen in 2011, 2015, and 2018, to name a few.

Today's dip seems to be driven by a couple of things. First, AVGO's revenue mix is shifting toward AI hardware with the success of its XPUs, and while top-line expansion looks great, hardware revenues tend to carry lower margins compared to software revenues. That's why management warned investors about an upcoming margin contraction on the gross level (~100 bps on a QoQ basis next quarter). As legacy segments look more tempting margins-wise, AVGO's forward valuation dropped, based on my understanding. Second, the rising AI-related backlog is currently spread out across just a few hyperscalers and LLM providers, making forward-looking projections less sustainable amid the general macro and geopolitical setup.

There is still 20%+ to go before AVGO reaches its 2020 sell-off level, but I don't think it's reasonable for long-term investors to wait for such a massive meltdown. The stock has already started to look attractive in terms of its valuation. The fundamental case for buying Broadcom - its dominant role as a global custom AI platform enabler - has only strengthened in recent months, in my opinion. I see a very comfortable upside potential ahead, and that's why I'm issuing a Buy recommendation.

Broadcom's XPU Acceleration And Ethernet's Victory

It's a well-known fact that Broadcom holds about 60% of the custom AI accelerators market, which is why the rise of this tech niche has been driving the firm's finances at a very rapid pace for the past quarters. From AVGO's fiscal Q1 2026 report, we see that AI semis revenues went up by 106% YoY and amounted to ~$8.40 billion, while for the next quarter, management sees $10.70 billion. That's going to be a 140% YoY expansion. The most important thing here is the internal projections that this demand boost isn't going to stop just in 2026 - AVGO has a clear path toward 2027 beyond just TPUs from.

For Google, we continue our trajectory of growth in '26 with strong demand for the seventh-generation Ironwood TPU. In 2027 and beyond, we expect to see even stronger demand from next generations of TPU. For Anthropic, we are off to a very good start in 2026 for 1 gigawatt of TPU compute. And for '27, this demand is expected to surge in excess of 3 gigawatts of compute. Our XPU franchise, I should add, extends beyond TPUs.

AVGO's Q1 earnings call

In addition to Anthropic mentioned above, we know that OpenAI has become the 6th customer (among major ones), planning to deploy their first generation XPU in volume in 2027 at over 1 GW of compute capacity.

Rounding off for customers 4 and 5, we see strong shipments this year and which we expect to more than double in 2027. We also now have a sixth customer. We expect OpenAI deploying in volume their first-generation XPU in 2027 at over 1 gigawatt of compute capacity. Let me take a second to emphasize our collaboration with these 6 customers to develop AI XPUs is deep, strategic and multiyear

AVGO's Q1 earnings call

If you follow the news tape, you probably know that OpenAI raised another financing round a few days ago, this time raising $120 billion at an $852 billion post-money valuation. It's important in today's AVGO discussion because the new capital inflow directly de-risks all the projects that OpenAI committed to beforehand, including those related to the AVGO deals. Looking at March 2026 price action, I don't see it properly priced in by the market in AVGO's valuation just yet.

If I were a Wall Street analysts on the sell-side, I wouldn't lower my estimate on AVGO's XPUs production and commercialization beyond 2026. But what's also important in Broadcom's growth story is its networking business.

AI models logically scale from single servers to clusters with thousands of nodes that must work reliably. Previously, Nvidia (NVDA) had an edge here with its InfiniBand solution, but today, we're witnessing a shift back to open Ethernet, where AVGO has better positioning, in my opinion. Since June 2025, when Ethernet launched a specification that "reconstructs the network stack to achieve InfiniBand-like performance", the prospects of its market share gains have materially improved. In the next few years, it's going to eat away at InfiniBand's shares. AVGO, as one of the biggest players here, should logically enjoy the whole move up.

On networking, our technology is really very, very uniquely positioning us to help our customers and more than our customers, even customers using general purpose GPUs, not just XPUs, which is that if you are running -- trying to create LLMs and running -- creating your own AI data centers and designing it, architecting it, you truly want larger and larger domains or clusters for -- and you really want to connect XPUs to XPUs directly where you can. And the best way to do that is to use direct attach copper. That's the lowest latency, lowest power and lowest cost. So you want to keep doing that, especially in scale-up as long as possible.

AVGO's Q1 earnings call

As long as AI builders and hyperscalers keep spending, AVGO's AI revenue growth on both the XPUs and networking side should continue piling up

Broadcom projects $100 billion in AI chips revenues for 2027 alone. Compared to FY2025's $20 billion in consolidated AI revenues, it's a massive increase, which is unlikely to lose its growth steam beyond 2027. A lot of people fear that the supply chains will ruin these plans, but the fact is that AVGO has already fully secured it. According to the management's commentaries, the needed wafers from TSMC (TSM), HBMs, and advanced packaging substrates are secured for the years 2026 through 2028, so I see limited downside here.

We went and secured all the elements Hock talked about. And when we secure this, it requires investments with these partners, sometimes developing not just more capacity but the right technology and capacity for that. So we have to go secure it for multiple years and we're probably -- you're right, we're probably the first one to secure that up to '28 or beyond.

AVGO's Q1 earnings call

So, even if YoY growth in AI CAPEX slows down to low double digits by 2028 (or even high single digits, we can't really know), Broadcom's supplies of both XPUs and Ethernet networking products and solutions will be very high on the "to-buy" list. I don't see any major demand disruption in the foreseeable future.

More Comfortable Valuation Levels

It might seem from Seeking Alpha's Quant system data that AVGO's valuation hasn't bottomed yet, based on classical P/E or EV/Sales ratios.

However, I believe we should prioritize the growth-weighted metrics like the forward PEG ratio. On a non-GAAP basis, the stock's forward PEG ratio is only 0.67x, as you can see above. Compared to the sector's median figure of 1.30x, AVGO looks clearly undervalued (by over 48%).

When we take a look at the consensus estimates for years ahead, we see that analysts predict a 5-year CAGR of 25.71%, with most of the growth skewed to the next 2 years (2026-2028). So, even at FY2028 EPS year-over-year growth rate of 24.30%, the market's views imply a PEG of ~0.58x, so the multiple contraction is effectively rolling to the next couple of years. I don't see it happening if AVGO executes on its medium-term plans.

Let's assume that the AI hype fades away by 2028, and AVGO starts to trade at its comfortable P/E range already in 2027, closer to the center. From 2010 to 2024, YCharts shows this center at 41.10x, but I'll take 30x because of the larger scale that naturally limits growth rates. AVGO's P/E ratio can drop below the 30x level, of course, but I believe this multiple is likely to be retained because the firm's moat is only getting wider amid the rising role in custom AI accelerators and Ethernet.

So, at 30x FY2027 earnings, assuming the current FY2027 EPS consensus is correct, AVGO's fair value per share arrives at $535.2/share. The stock looks undervalued by over 70% if these assumptions play out.

Risks To My Views

Since I've just laid out my base case valuation assumptions, let's stress-test them right away. There's a chance that shortages of some critical components may lead to delayed shipments, lower recognized revenues, and hence below-consensus EPS by the end of 2027. AVGO might miss the current FY2027 EPS by 10%. The market might panic, or simply adjust the FY2027 P/E multiple to 15-20x. If all this plays out, AVGO's fair value per share would come in at only $281/share, which is about 11% below today's stock price.

There's another risk that I'm particularly worried about - the rise of hardware revenues in the mix might push margins down far more than the 100 bps that management guided for. In this case, my above stress testing would quickly become the base case scenario.

Conclusion

It's hard to be bearish on Broadcom stock after its recent sell-off that sent the price almost 24% off all-time highs with no fundamental reason for that. There might be some profit taking on top, but as long as the business is fine and growing well, with clear prospects ahead, I think it's time to buy, not sell. The upside potential I calculated above only proves this statement of mine.

data.title: Outlining The Challenges And My 'Hold' With Albertsons
data.description:
I happily cover consumer staples. Over the years, I've often held significant positions in many staples companies, including but not limited to Swedish and local businesses like Axfood (AXFOY) and ICA (no longer publicly listed) as well as regional and EU-wide businesses like Ahold Delhaize (ADRNY). All of these investments, over time, have been successes - but I am currently not invested in any of them due to causes of valuation.

In this article, I will look at US-based grocer Albertsons, technically a solid company, but as of late not exactly trading at, or seeing significant high or impressive trends. I will discover the reasons for this. Going by the company's numerous following analysts and the ratings they have given the company, the conveyed image is that this is a severe miscalculation and, in essence, an "incorrect" price for the company. This drawdown in price has left the grocer trading at less than 8.2x P/E, when many peers and other grocers are trading at valuations that move well into 15-17x P/E, so about twice as high or even more.

So is it a matter of the analysts missing some key risk, or is it the market misapplying a significant discount multiple to what isn't really a discountable risk? I have, over the years of my analyst activity, seen both of these things happen, so it's a good thing in this case to see what I believe to be the truth of the matter.

In essence, and looking from a bird's-eye view, there's absolutely nothing wrong with Albertsons. The company is one of the largest food and drug retailers in the entire nation. The company operates well over 2,200 stores, which makes it bigger in size than any Swedish investment in the sector I have ever made. It has several well-known brands under its banner, including things like Albertsons, Safeway, Vons, and Jewel-Osco.

While grocers are facing structural, operational, and financial challenges and need to meet them, it seems to me at first glance that the company should have no trouble doing just that.

So let's see what a second glance brings us.

Albertsons - Looking at fundamentals and issues

The first thing I do when I see a company facing the sort of issues that this company seems to is a back-to-basics approach - I look at the company's filings in detail. And, to find what the market may be seeing, we don't need to go a whole lot further than this either. The company is a business whose balance sheet is in tatters (although that might be a bit of a strong word, given BB+), due to debt. The company's issues can be expressed with a variety of debt and cash-related metrics, or the poor state of them. About $2.5B in Equity as of LTM is a very good place to start, relative to over $24.5B in liabilities.

Generally speaking, when a company has an equity ratio like this, we can conclude that something has gone wrong. A company with very little relative equity left has gone through periods of distress or restructuring. It is one of the primary indicators of insolvency. The only way out of it, barring capital injections or new investments, is if the company has mammoth-type cash flows to handle its debts. It's neither impossible nor unheard of for a company in this situation to see a turnaround - it's just rare.

So generally speaking, the market is right to put a significant discount on a business in this state. Going forward, to even be considered attractive, Albertsons needs to deliver significant appeal and value. The company's tangible book value is negative.

Albertsons has $15B+ of net debt (about $8B of which is long-term), and unfortunately, this number has not been getting significantly smaller for some years. This tells me that the company's interest expense is likely high - and indeed, while this line item is not at record numbers, it still consumes more than 15% of the company's operating income on an annual basis. After everything said and done, Albertsons manages a net margin of 1.8%. This is low for a grocer, but it should also be noted that this is a turnaround from the last few years.

So, it's also fair to say that things for Albertsons have been significantly worse than we're currently seeing them. This is something that we can also see in other metrics. It doesn't take care of the debt problem, but I would argue that it means the company is not in immediate problems at this very minute. FCF, while not monumental, is about $500M, which is at least positive and okay, and the company has $200M of cash on hand at the end of LTM.

SA analysts, Wall Street, and Quant all have the company at a positive "BUY" valuation - so is this a company in an incredible turnaround?

Market valuation trends suggest no.

The reason Albertsons has so much debt is related to its history. It was the target for LBO's, one particular one with Cerberus, that bought the company in its bid to restructure grocery companies. Albertsons was forced to pay a large special dividend, and has been financing operations with bonds, debt and other costs that once were part of its failed ambition to merge with Kroger (KR), a grocer in significantly better shape than Albertsons. In essence, the reason Albertsons is in such bad financial shape is a failed and long legal process. The $4B dividend paid in 2024 further emptied company cash reserves, leaving it as it is today, with essentially a negative book value or tangible share price.

The company is refinancing notes and debt to handle it, mainly for increased interest costs. Issuance of new senior notes to cover higher-interest debt might make this a net neutral, but even the 3x or below 3x targeted by the company is a relatively high ratio of debt for a grocer. The fact that the recent issuances were made at close to 6% interest is an implication that borrowing costs are going to rise. Albertsons has also used, historically, both sale-leaseback and other "moves" to try and reduce its leverage.

The company's saving grace is that even with its massive mountain of debt relative to its equity, Albertsons is profitable as a business - and I believe likely to remain so. Its market position in key geographies is solid, and likely to remain positive going forward. It would also be completely unfair to say that Albertsons' management doesn't know what it has to do. In fact, management is doing it - and by some metrics, it's doing it well.

So while AEPS is on a negative trajectory, and the company has certainly underperformed the market as of late, there are signs that things could rise. After all, we're talking about a grocer - there are few "safer" things to invest in than food retail. And the company's margins are all moving into what I would consider to be a decent direction.

Two things bother me - and I preface this by saying this is obviously my personal opinion. It's my view that, given the company's strained balance sheet, ACI has no business paying any dividend, least of all 3.4% of it. This dividend takes away over $330M per year of cash flow. This is money that should, by all appearances, be used to pay down debt and rebuild the company's balance sheet and equity. This is obviously small relative to the (heavily criticized) special dividend that was paid in 2023, but it still is, in my view, an irresponsible dividend given the company's current health.

It's also completely premature to say that the risks in this company are gone. Albertsons is facing significant issues. The fallout from the merger is all but over, and the margins that we see speak of intense competition with larger, better-capitalized, and better-managed companies like Kroger and Walmart (WMT), not to mention players like Amazon (AMZN). Furthermore, while the company is on a savings spree, this savings spree is also likely to bite deeply into revenues, due to the closures of many stores in SoCal.

Then there is the legal risk associated with Albertsons following the failed merger with Kroger. Albertsons is seeking $600M in damages. The way that the cases related to the merger have gone so far is that Kroger has been settling with, or being accused by, states in their role for the merger, but it's impossible to say the eventual outcome of this.

I say therefore that the decline and the current valuation pressure are well put, and I don't see a turnaround to be likely going forward. Why do I not consider a turnaround likely?

Valuation for the company - an unattractive combination of fundamentals

It goes without saying that Albertsons presents unattractively from a fundamental perspective. Its positive earnings and free cash flow are measured relatively against its debt, and its ability to pay down that debt, and over time to become better capitalized once again. I do not believe it to be an exaggeration to say that the company has years and years of work to do before being ready to be called fundamentally attractive again.

But I will say that Albertsons does have the fundamentals and the positioning to get there. Recent 3Q25 results do some work in confirming this upside, seeing significant digital sales growth.

However, at the same time, the company is a share buyback program, which, while potentially technically cheap shares, was not done at a time I consider good for the company. The fact that the company missed its own revenue estimate during the period is, in fact, a bigger warning sign for me than the successes in earnings.

I argue that Albertsons, as an independent entity, is much less equipped to compete with larger ones again. I further submit to you that in the event of a $600M payment, the company would have even more problems with debt. And while I know some analysts would not consider capital leases debt on the level of long-term debt (and usually I give this argument some validity), in this case, I would consider the company's $6B capital leases a fair addition to its debt. Why?

Because ACI, as a lessee, has assumed ownership risks, including Maintenance CapEx and taxes, and under debt-like commitments, it has a high risk of not being paid. So, it's not just a balance sheet adjustment - it impacts my purported valuation for the company.

I also believe that Albertsons' pricing strategy, of typically charging 12-15% more than its competitors, is part of the reason that it has been able to raise its margins and cash flows as much as it has. I do not believe this to be a maintainable strategy in the current economic macro. Further sales-related strategies that the company is engaging in include growing digital and pharma, but the problem is that these sales are typically of much lower quality and stickiness than traditional grocery sales - they are lower margin sales, which might lead to further pressure.

While I forecast a 3-4% AEPS growth for the company to be likely over time, this does nothing to take away from the company's subpar fundamentals. I point to historical underperformance, rising debt costs, and rates in conjunction with a BB+ rating. I forecast net income to decline, citing adjusted EBITDA down and forecasts down, signifying margin pressure. I believe the company is in a sub-par position relative to almost any other grocer.

The negative tangible book value and extremely low equity lead me to say that it would be investing in a company that, as of April 2026, is still somewhat "hollowed out". While returns and alpha are possible, I see better and safer returns, including better dividends, by investing in different companies from here on out.

Because of its significant debt, the company's uncertainty, and the asset-heavy nature of the company, I for once consider book value a good indicator for the company's price target. The company's current LTM book value is $4.87. The average book value multiple in this sector is about 3x, which would come to $14.61 - but I'd want to discount the company for its negative tangible by about 25%, bringing the PT to $11/share. This may be excessively negative - so if you want to stay somewhat more positive, I'd just omit my discounting, bringing the PT at least below $15/share.

But the result is that in any case, the company is not cheap enough here. I therefore say "HOLD".

Thesis

Albertsons is a grocer with a problematic balance sheet but profitable operations. At the right price, I would be interested - but based on the company's equity ratios, I use only book value assumptions to determine this, and discounted ones at that. Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield (which I consider to be somewhat irresponsible), I would put a $11/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing.

The company doesn't, as such, fulfil the second or third criteria, but based on its market position and the fact that I do not view bankruptcy as likely, I consider it at least baseline objectively qualitative - even if a turnaround or anything approaching investability (unless bought at cheap $11 or below) is far off.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 1 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 1/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $11/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Hess Midstream: Time To Reap The Rewards, But Not Immune To Macro Pressures
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Hess Midstream LP (HESM), I highlighted their strong cash flow and resilient financials despite recent pressure resulting from macro issues, supporting their solid yield.

Following a strong end of the year and a pivot away from their investments into a focus on capital returns and balance sheet improvements, HESM remains a Buy despite returning ~20% since then, proving their resilience in the face of macro uncertainty, despite potential near-term inflationary pressure, still trading at a solid valuation that allows them to pay a strong, competitive, and sustainable yield.

Time to Reap The Rewards

HESM reported a strong end of the year, beating the market’s EPS estimates despite missing the revenue ones, with the Adj. FCF reaching $779 million, marking the end of their CAPEX-intensive cycle and preparing to enjoy significant benefits from their investments and a significant drop in CAPEX back to normal levels.

After investing significant amounts over the past couple of years, building their infrastructure in an effort to keep up with rising upstream production needs and building their capacity for the long-term, HESM is now pivoting towards capital returns, targeting 5% distribution growth through 2028, with potential for buybacks and debt repayments on top. In fact, about a month ago, HESM announced signing $60 million worth of repurchases from the sponsor and the public, which would bring the ownership to ~62.2% for the public and ~37.8% for Chevron (CVX), which bought Hess Corporation not long ago.

As a result, HESM expects their Adjusted FCF to grow to $850 million to $900 million and then grow at a 10% annual growth rate from that point to 2028, expecting their distribution growth to be funded even at minimum volume commitment levels, which is very attractive.

By 2028, they expect to generate roughly $1 billion in Adj. FCF after distributions (expected to pay $665 million in 2026, for a yield of ~8.4% and a payout ratio of 76%), aiming at decreasing its long-term leverage to <3.0x Adj. EBITDA (<3.0 expected in 2026, <2.5 expected in 2027 and 2028).

Financially, based on HESM’s latest report, we see an overall normal position for a company like this, and even though the debt is growing (drawn on their revolving credit facility) to fund their recent expansion, that’s before the company’s targets of reducing their leverage.

As we can see, the company’s debt schedule is solid, and their fixed-rate senior notes come at 4.25% to 5.875% coupons, which is not terrible for a midstream, especially in the current environment, and given the lack of debt this year and manageable facilities being due in 2027, they could even benefit from potential drops in interest rates by the time they have to refinance their larger maturities.

HESM is expected to announce its Q1 results on April 27, 2026, with the market expecting Normalized EPS of $0.67 and a revenue of ~$389.88 million. Despite the ongoing pivot and macro uncertainty, I don't expect some major surprise (positive or negative), and I'll pay close attention especially to the company's insights into the environment, as they generally offer a good idea into what's going on especially with Chevron.

Resilient Doesn’t Mean Immune

Although the stock has been very stable despite the significant volatility seen in the market over the past month as a result of the Iran conflict (further proving their resilience to this type of macro factors), they are still an LP at the end of the day, with the distributions being one of the main factors behind this investment.

As warned by Powell and the ECB’s leader recently, we could be in for a significant inflationary shock caused the conflict and potentially prolonged by the damage done by Iran’s counterattacks against its neighbors, potentially leading to these war premiums being reflected in pump prices and eventually spread into broader ones in the near-term.

As a result, central banks could decide to pause the cuts or even hike rates, which is ultimately their tool to fight rising inflation, increasing borrowing costs, diminishing discretionary budgets and slowing down the economy.

This can then strengthen the dollar (the opposite of rate cuts) and result in higher treasury yields while the market would also demand a better reward for the level of risk it’s taking given the potential impact on long-term expectations that go into these yields, potentially affecting HESM’s valuation as a result of the market having to adjust it to the new theoretically risk-free rate.

Meanwhile, the SOFR could also stagnate or even rise as a result, which can directly affect HESM’s costs of debt, while the 8% yield on their shares already makes it very tough for them to issue in order to fund investments efficiently, although they expect CAPEX to drop.

Still, regardless of this near-term volatility that might even open better entry levels in HESM, the company remains in a great position to benefit from long-term trends such as the shift towards natural gas, Bakken maturing and increasing gas-to-oil ratios (management expects gas throughput volumes to grow at ~1.5% per year through 2028), with HESM itself expecting gas processing/gathering to represent ~75% of their total affiliate revenues from 2026 through 2028 excluding pass-through revenues (similar to the targeted Gross Adjusted EBITDA Margin), while they built their infrastructure to benefit from these and North Dakota’s gas capture needs.

Fellow analyst Seeking Profits also highlighted very recently how the “Iran Conflict Reduces Bakken Production Fears,” which can be yet another point behind their stable performance, helping alleviate the fear generated by Chevron reducing their drilling activity in the Bakken as oil prices fell before this conflict, which war premium can now help offset for the foreseeable future. The company’s CEO also talked about Chevron’s recent boe/d target during their Q4 Earnings Call, stating:

In terms of the 200,000, I mean, no change there. You just heard Chevron recently just Friday on their call, reiterating the 200,000 barrels a day target with continued optimization program. (...) And all of this is consistent, as we said, with Chevron's development plan that targets 200,000 BOE per day with continued opportunities for optimization as well as 95% MVC revenue protection this year in 2026 and 90% MVC revenue protection in 2027. So we've talked a lot about changes as a result of the transition, but I think it's important to highlight that we continue to have the elements of visibility and consistency, the shareholder returns and balance sheet strength that have been and continue to be the hallmark of Hess Midstream and really a differentiator in the strategy. So when we talk about the long term for Hess Midstream, while things have changed, it's really a lot more of the same unique combination that has always been our hallmark.

Meanwhile, AI, data centers, and similar energy-intensive trends can also provide more investing opportunities for HESM or structurally more volume in the longer-term despite near-term structural bottlenecks in capacity/limitations, potentially also standing to benefit from Chevron's aggressive AI positioning.

Risks Reiterated

Although they’ve proven to be more stable, the company still depends on the economy at the end of the day, and a weak natural gas market can result in weaker performance at least in the near-term given the potential quick resolution of the Iran conflict (similar to how the pressure from lower oil prices affecting CVX’s drilling activity caused their fall not long ago), which can add pressure on their distributions.

Meanwhile, if the conflict isn’t over fast, the risk of inflationary shocks are even higher, potentially affecting treasury yields and subsequently HESM’s valuation, as the opportunity cost would change.

Valuation

For HESM’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from their guidance of $875 million in Adj. FCF in 2026, I’ll assume they can grow this by $87.5 million per year through 2028, while also including $325 million worth of debt repayments in 2027 (doesn’t have to be all at once, but still) to take into account their target of reducing their leverage to <2.5x.

This would be followed by a 3.5% CAGR until year 10, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a discount rate of 12.5% to demand a solid margin of safety, as well as 207.69 million diluted shares outstanding (Class A + B) as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $9.99 billion, for an intrinsic value of $48.08 per unit, which is still above the current $38.68 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

HESM is a Buy, backed by a solid business with a good foundation and strong distributions that can benefit from long-term tailwinds, pivoting back into focusing on capital returns.

There are risks to consider given the environment and potential for a slowdown, but the overall reward even if the environment gets worse should be solid at current levels.

data.title: UPS: Rising Oil Prices Reveal How Fragile The Carrier's Profitability Levels Remain
data.description:
Good investments have to survive the test of time. While many companies perform well during good times, an extended sell-off is often a good way to stress-test a financial choice. Even though economic growth rates remain stable, most corporations are no longer able to rely on what have shown to be unreliable price increases over the last several years, and many of these companies are seeing minimal organic growth as well.

Few corporations have struggled more since COVID and the pandemic era shutdowns ended than United Parcel Services (UPS). The carrier saw record-high revenues in 2021 and 2022, when UPS made nearly $100 billion that year.

UPS has offered investors total returns of negative 29.26 percent over the last 5 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 75.17 percent during the same time period. The carrier has consistently and significantly underperformed the broader indexes for several years now.

I last wrote about the United Parcel Services in January of 2026, and I rated the company a strong sell. Today, I am reiterating my strong sell rating of the carrier. The company's turnaround plan is not showing substantive progress of achieving the core goal of both increasing margins while also driving substantive revenue and earnings growth. The recent price shock in the oil market also further shows how vulnerable the company's fragile profitability levels are to rising costs as well. UPS still looks overvalued.

UPS reported fourth-quarter earnings per share normalized actual $2.38 and revenue actual of $24.48 billion, beating lowered expectations of nearly $24 billion and $2.20 a share. The United Parcel Services gets nearly 76 percent of the company's revenues from the US market and 67 percent of revenues from domestic shipping within the United States. The company also stated domestic revenue per piece increased by 8.3 percent while the carrier's volumes predictably declined as expected, but the drop in volume in part led to lower revenues on a year-to-year basis by nearly $1.4 billion. UPS's operating margin was 8.5 percent within this region.

The most important metrics for the carrier are revenue per piece and shipping volumes. While UPS was able to drive international revenue growth of 2.5 percent on a year-to-year basis, primarily by increasing revenue per piece by 7.1 percent. The company's international operating margin was 17.5 percent.

UPS also guided to revenue in 2026 of $89.7 billion, and analysts are expecting the carrier to earn between $7.07 and $7.21 a share for this year. Management also stated the company plans to spend nearly $3 billion this year as part of a plan to increase efficiency by moving more to automation. The carrier closed 93 buildings in 2025 as well.

Even though UPS has been able to drive margin expansion by reducing volumes and focusing on higher-end shipments, the company's plan is still not driving any real or substantive revenue growth in the critically important US market. UPS saw a 3.2 percent decline in year-over-year revenue in the United States primarily because the carrier saw average daily volume fall by 10.8 percent while margins in this market only increased by 8.3 percent. Management's turnaround plan is still not driving any revenue growth in the United States market, where the company gets nearly two-thirds of the carrier's overall earnings.

UPS's revenues peaked in September of 2022 at nearly $101 billion, and the company's 12-month trailing revenues have been in consistent decline since that time.

The company's 12-month trailing earnings per share have also been in consistent decline since December of 2022, when UPS earned $13.20 a share on a 12-month trailing basis.

UPS's overall net margins have also risen only slightly over the last year despite the company's focus on profitability. The company's net margin of 6.28 percent remains below the 10-year average for the carrier as well.

While UPS remains committed to returning cash to shareholders with buybacks and the dividend, which management has made clear the company is not going to cut, the company's long-term debt has risen significantly over the last decade as well. While the carrier returned $6.4 billion to shareholders in 2025 with buybacks and dividends, UPS has approximately $1.3 billion remaining that was authorized for share buybacks from a 2023 plan. The company expects to generate nearly $6.5 billion in free cash flow this year.

UPS currently has nearly $22.8 billion in long-term debt despite having a market cap of just $83.19 billion.

The carrier is also impacted by rising fuel costs, which have resulted from the current conflict between the US and Iran. The company relied on purchased transportation and third-party carriers last year, which made up nearly 20 percent of UPS's costs, and those expenses will obviously rise with diesel and fuel prices increasing. The United Postal Service is still seeing revenues decline while earnings and margins stagnate. These current challenges make the carrier's business model vulnerable to even moderate margin compression, which should result from rising energy prices.

This is why UPS looks overvalued at 14x expected forward earnings. Even though the company's peer, FedEx (FDX) corporation, trades at 20.85x projected forward earnings, this carrier has consistently shown substantive earnings growth over the last 6 quarters expected to more than double the company's earnings by 2031. While UPS's 5-year average valuation is 15.80x projected forward earnings, UPS's earnings and revenue growth have flatlined right now, and analyst expectations for consistent double-digit earnings growth moving forward are likely too optimistic since the company has not been able to increase revenues in key domestic markets by focusing on higher-margin shipments as part of the carrier's turnaround plan.

UPS is expected to grow revenues at just 4-6 percent over the next five years, and while some analysts are projecting low double-digit earnings growth moving forward, the company's falling shipping volumes has offset the increase in margins management's plan is focusing on. The carrier continues to see stagnant earnings per share as the company face challenges and difficult comparisons to recent record earnings coming out of COVID.

Still, all investment theses have risks, and UPS continues to focus on moving towards automation to reduce labor costs and streamline operations. The company's plan may eventually drive more substantive margin expansion. The war with Iran and oil prices are also volatile, an end to the conflict could see energy costs return to prewar levels. The carrier has also been able to maintain the dividend even during the recent turnaround plan. Management has clearly shown that the company is committed to maximizing shareholder returns with buybacks and payouts.

UPS was one of the better-performing companies in the market for some time, but the carrier has not found a way to drive significant and consistent revenue and earnings growth over the last 3 years, and management's current plan is facing challenges. While United Parcel Services has driven margin expansion domestically and earnings growth overseas, management is still struggling to deliver substantive revenue and earnings growth in the key US market.

data.title: Rigetti: Still Overvalued But Fundamentals Have Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Rigetti Computing (RGTI) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail, explained why I didn’t understand all the excitement about the company, and why I did consider it a strong sell. Since then, the stock has fallen by a 50% and has almost reached my $11 fair value; however, most of this fall is due to a reduction in the quantum valuations, which is why I further reduced its fair value to $8 per share.

Despite this I upgrade the stock to a sell because lately it is achieving more contracts.

Background

Rigetti Computing is a quantum computing company founded in 2013. The company follows a full stack approach, developing superconducting qubit based processors in its in house Fab-1, the only dedicated and integrated quantum foundry, and selling cloud access through Quantum Cloud Services.

Rigetti bases its quantum technology on superconducting qubits, as it considers that offer fast gate speeds and modular architectures based on semiconductor manufacturing. However, those advantages still come with meaningful disadvantages around coherence, complexity and temperature requirements because to achieve superconducting qubits, the electrical circuits must be cooled to near absolute zero.

These difficulties have already been showed by several delays with the last one this year. In January management delayed its Cepheus-1-108Q and said the system was expected to reach general availability around the end of the first quarter of 2026. Even during the Q4 conference call at the beginning of March management said:

Our near-term priority is completing deployment of the 108-qubit system at 99.5% median 2-qubit gate fidelity, which we expect around the end of March. Beyond that, our focus is to deploy a system with more than 150-qubits with an anticipated 99.7% median 2-qubit gate fidelity around end of December 2026.

Given that it is now April and the company has not announced that milestone, it is fair to assume the timeline has been delayed again, and I expect that the 150 qubits system by December 2026 will also be delayed.

However, that does not mean there has been no progress as the company has achieved some new contracts. This increase in contract announcements is important because one of the original bearish points was the lack of meaningful commercial traction; however, for now the demand is still concentrated in early stage research deployments for universities and research institutions, keeping revenues small and volatile.

Financial Position and Valuation

Rigetti has a comfortable financial position with around $590 million in cash or equivalents and no debt at the end of Q4. The increase in cash compared to Q3 was due to the exercise of 4 million warrants, which generated proceeds of around $45 million. However, the Q4 cash burn was approximately $14 million as the last quarter did not show a meaningful improvement in the income statement.

Rigetti reported Q4 2025 revenue of $1.9 million, which is the same as the previous quarter and lower than previous year’s $2.3 million. This is because the company is still very dependent on the timing of deliveries of its few contracts.

While I am more optimistic about 2026 revenues due to the revenue recognition of two contracts, valuation remains too expensive.

In the Q4 conference call the CEO said that the revenue recognition for the $5.7 million Novera sales would be split between the first and second quarters of 2026, while the $8.4 million C-DAC order is expected to be recognized in the second semester. Those two contracts alone will represent more than the company’s 2025 revenue.

However, even assuming that the 2026 revenue reaches $17.5 million, its $4.6 billion valuation is difficult to justify.

For valuing the leader in the sector IonQ (IONQ), I used a forward 100xP/S ratio (though I believe Infleqtion (INFQ) could be the leader in the future). I don’t believe Rigetti deserves a premium over IonQ, so if I value the company with a 100xP/S, its valuation should be $1.75 billion, or around $5 per share.

However, for some reason Rigetti tends to trade at a premium, even assuming a 150x P/S its market cap should be $2.6 billion, implying a fair value around $8 per share.

Risks

The main risk of shorting Rigetti is that if interest in quantum stocks rises again, the stock will rise regardless of fundamentals. Valuation has come down a lot as it was trading above 1,000xP/S when I published the initial article, but it provides perspective where it could go.

In the short term there is also the risk that the market rises following the announcement of the 108 qubit system which could help change the narrative to a more positive one.

Finally, if the positive contract momentum continues it could be perceived positively by the market, potentially leading to a higher valuation.

Conclusion

Rigetti is still too expensive given the future revenues it could achieve in the medium term and the continued delays in its products. The company has still to demonstrate that it could scale its quantum technology before it deserves any premium valuation.

Despite nearly reaching my previous fair value, quantum stocks have seen their ratios come down meaningfully so I have updated my fair value to $8 per share, still representing a substantial downside.

However, I am upgrading the stock rating to a sell from a strong sell due to higher contract momentum.

data.title: Robinhood: World-Class Entry Point (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Robinhood (HOOD) stock is down by -25% since my last analysis, in which I issued a Hold rating on the company. However, now there's evidence that we could be set up at world-class entry point. I admit that the equity is valued speculatively, but I'm willing to overlook that on the premise that HOOD stock looks to track Bitcoin (BTC) broadly, as well as software. Therefore, once the crypto winter ends, I see it as quite logical that sentiment for HOOD stock will begin to rise. It's not a value investment, but it does have decent growth and is trading down from its prior highs significantly. To be specific, Hood is 55% below its all-time high. I rate the stock a Buy.

Business Fundamentals

Robinhood's growth positioning is elite, even if the fundamental value isn't. FY25 revenue grew 52%, adjusted EBITDA grew 76%, and adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 8 percentage points. 11 of the company's businesses were already above a $100M annualized run-rate by the end of 2025.

Furthermore, platform assets in FY25 were up by about 67% year-over-year (not all organic), with last-twelve-month net deposits compounding at about a 35% rate relative to the prior asset base. Moreover, Q4 ARPU increased by 16%, Gold subscribers grew by 58%, and retirement AUC grew 102%. This is fundamentally well-diversified business that is growing across several fronts.

Robinhood stock trades with crypto because the market still sees it as a liquid crypto-adjacent risk-appetite proxy. Crypto was about 20% of Robinhood's 2025 revenue, and its crypto market share rose 63% year-over-year. Unfortunately, unlike a Bitcoin treasury like Strategy (MSTR), or direct crypto, Robinhood stock is currently trading like a speculative sentiment asset even though it has a reliable and diversified operating business.

Valuation

Most readers are not going to like this valuation strategy, but I'm going to do it anyway because you have to break a few traditions to beat the herd who can't live without them. Even though HOOD has an SA Quant "Sell" rating, and a Quant valuation grade of D-, I'm bullish on the technicals. I know, I know, that's not a reliable long-term strategy for fundamentalists. But it has its place in a portfolio geared toward convexity and portfolio insurance in other vectors. For example, with 37% in cash and cash equivalents, even if a recession occurred now, which I don't think it will, I'd be well-off in the downturn to deploy into value. I can afford to own some high-convexity "speculative" positions like HOOD.

The weekly RSI has just closed-in on essentially oversold. The price action of HOOD has also mimicked BTC broadly. And the business, though fundamentally "overvalued," is growing well, popular with customers, and has a reliable long-term trajectory. To me, these are the simple ingredients of a strong trade. I see it as very likely that HOOD will rise when BTC rises. It's that simple.

We can argue all we want about the "more technical aspects," the "balance sheet," the "intrinsic discounted value of future cash flows," and all other parameters, which I already understand. It won't make you any more cash! Instead, I come back to basics. Relative value, RSIs, growth trajectory, and then if I want to be conservative, I look for a margin of safety. HOOD is not conservative.

If the company delivers $3 in normalized EPS in December 2027, then the stock price will likely be $105 in December 2026 if the forward P/E is 35, up only marginally from the present 31.5. In other words, as the stock is now $69, a +52% return.

Risks

The HOOD = BTC proxy narrative is less clean that it sounds on paper in reality. Crypto was 20% of FY25 revenue, and management still maintains that the business is diversifying across many lines. If diversification keeps working, HOOD may stop giving the clean one-for-one BTC beta this thesis is relying on, but I still thinks it's valid for now.

Right now, the revenue model is still cyclical and concentrated, with PFOF economics. 59% of FY25 revenue was transaction-based, and 55% of total net revenue consisted of transaction-based revenues paid by market makers and exchanges. Citadel alone represented 13% of net revenue. This creates volatility risks in the price and makes a low entry point, like now, more opportune.

Finally, management guided 2026 adjusted opex and SBC to $2.6B-$2.7B, up 18% at the midpoint, with more than half of that growth going into new and scaling businesses. If adoption lags, the multiple can compress before the revenue shows up and cap the upside thesis presented here.

Conclusion: Buy

Is HOOD one of the best stocks on the market? No, not in my opinion. But is it one of the best entry points right now for somewhat speculative returns that are not heavy on margin of safety but high on technical positive probability outcome? Yes. Or to be more blunt, HOOD mimics crypto, and crypto winter is over soon and we'll be heading into spring.

Some people might not like the stock, and that's fine. You stick to your discounted cash flows and your advanced technical analysis, that's fine. If it works for you, it works for you. This is what works for me. We're all entitled to our own views and strategies. And I've been hitting 50-60% annual returns recently, so I'm not planning on changing course any time soon. Onwards and upwards.

On a closing and more positive note, I'm fond of HOOD's management. After watching several interviews with the CEO, I'm convinced that the business is in good hands. Robinhood isn't my main broker, but I do use it for one portfolio, and you guessed it, it's the one most heavily oriented to crypto. In fact, it's a crypto treasury portfolio. HOOD does the job well. The company's financial statements and forward growth look strong, and the technicals on the stock price right now look primed for a world-class entry point.

data.title: Palantir: Winner Of The AI War
data.description:
Following the war in Iran, aerospace and defense stocks have not fared well but there is one company that has taken the spotlight in the war both in positive and negative sense and that company is Palantir Technologies (PLTR). The stock has gained 8.7% since my last report. In this report, I pay closer attention to how Palantir’s product stack is used as the current war in Iran provides large-scale evidence of how Palantir’s stack scales the war capabilities.

Note: War often comes with an unmeasurable amount of human tragedy and a fair bit of geopolitics. While I am cognizant of these factors, this report does not intend to discuss either the human tragedy or the politics behind the most recent combat action but aims to provide qualitative analysis of the role of Palantir’s product stack in the war.

Palantir’s Modular Set Up Shines

Palantir product suite is often misunderstood because it is not a set of isolated products, but a layered software stack where each component plays a distinct role. Understanding how these pieces connect is of paramount importance to understand the product value proposition.

  1. Palantir Foundry can be seen as the data backbone integrating data from disparate sources, builds a data model and enables analytics and model development.
  2. Palantir Gotham can be seen as the operational brain and operational decision platform fusing multiple intelligence streams into an operational picture allowing for mission planning and execution. Whereas Foundry organizes data, Gotham fuses it and turns its into actionable operations.
  3. Palantir Apollo allows for the continuous delivery of software even in disconnected environments.
  4. Pantar Artificial Intelligence Platform or AIP is the AI orchestration layer that sits on top of Foundry and Gotham and introduces AI-driven decision making.
  5. Maven Smart System is a mission-specific AI application built on top of the stack used for analyzing drone and sensor data, object detection and target tracking and prioritization.

So, we have full stack that integrates data through Foundry, provides operational real-time context through Gotham, runs AI enabled analysis and models through AIP while Maven performs specialized tasks and Apollo is the software distribution layer. Each part provides a key function. Maven Smart System is in focus in Iran, but it actually needs all modules to execute its tasks. Maven Smart Systems is the result of Project Maven which was launched in 2017 to accelerate adoption of machine learning and data integration in the U.S. military intelligence layer. In 2018, the purpose of the project was altered to AI-enabled analysis of video with a further step up towards combat commands.

The system capabilities have evolved over time with evolving AI implementation allowing the number of targets per day to rise from less than 100 to 1,000 after computer vision system implementation. Computer Vision System is simply processing images and video in a way a human would and assign targets based on that. Language learning models further increased the capability to 5,000 targets per day with future iterations expected to push target acquisition to 1,000 per hour. What it essentially does is removing the intelligence and decision making bottleneck and that allowed the US and Israel to strike 2,000 targets within 48 hours. As a reference, in the war in Iraq in 2003 human analysts were able to pin point about 100 targets per day on cycle times of 24-72 hrs. Through AI and automation and parallelized work flow, the US can now target over 1,000 targets per day clearly showing the shift from analysis and targeting to strike capacity.

In 2025, NATO also selected Maven and I believe it is not a weird thing that NATO selected Maven as Maven provides a real edge that especially in the early phase of the war can result in targeted strikes that can determine the outcome of a war.

There Is A Negative On Maven

One reason why target cycles were substantially longer 13 years ago was the human approach towards target setting as well as verification in a largely sequential set up. Maven is parallelized and can target much faster, but it also becomes more difficult to correct wrong target setting as also has happen in Iran. When wrong targets are being set, it bears the question who carries responsibility. Furthermore, because the high speed and automated character of Maven, it can also distance militaries from gravity of life-and-death decisions. So, there is the risk of automation bias trusting deeply on a system that may still occasionally mistarget. Other risks include perception of the authority of the platform leading to reduced willingness to challenge AI assigned targets as well as cognitive offloading and moral disengagement and deskilling.
